text: We present you a laparoscopic right amicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis in a medial to lateral approach in a standardized way, allowing the easy learning of the trainee.
Our patient was a sixty-six year old female previously streptomized by infraumbilical median laparotomy with a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the cecum proposed for a laparoscopic right amicolectomy.
In the preoperative CT scan is visible the neoplastic lesion in the sacral pole and the absence of metastatic lesions.

Four trochars are placed for the laparoscopic procedure: two 10mm trochars, one in the umbilicus for the laparoscopic optic and another suprapubic for the surgeon's left hand, and two 5mm trochars, one in the left iliac fossa for the surgeon's right hand and another in the upper left quadrant for the assistant.
Both the surgeon and the assistant were on the left side of the patient.
We outline the surgical procedure in eight fundamental steps that will be explained one by one throughout this video.
The surgeon confirms the location of the neoplastic lesion in the sacral pole and inspects the remaining abdominal cavity using a laparoscopic camera.
The surgeon exposes the ileocecal region and applies traction to the mesentery, making the ileocolic vessels clearly visible.
The surgeon initiates dissection to individualize the ileocolic vessels by creating a small opening in the mesentery inferior to these vessels.
The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg and left tilt orientation to enhance exposure, while the greater omentum is mobilized towards the liver for optimal surgical access.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and applies two hemostatic clips before sectioning the vessels using a bipolar electrosurgery device.
The surgeon creates the submisocolic space, manipulating the mesocolon to separate it from underlying structures, following the exposure of the ileocecal region.
The surgeon identifies the third portion of the duodenum, using it as a reference point to locate the correct dissection plane.
Sometimes an overview is needed in order that the dissection is as cranial as possible toward the liver.
The surgeon makes a small correction in the dissection path, adjusting the approach to remain aligned with the anatomical root.
The surgeon uses blunt traction and counter traction, aided by pneumoperitoneum, to facilitate the creation of the submesocolic space.
The surgeon continues dissection until reaching the liver, positioning the retroperitoneum below and exposing the posterior face of the mesocolon.
A gauze is placed in the submesocolic space, serving as a visual reference for future surgical steps.
The surgeon opens the visceral sheath of the mesocolic peritoneum, followed by the sectioning of the mesocolon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the right middle colic artery branch, applies two hemostatic clips, and sections the vessel using a bipolar electrosurgery device.
The surgeon continues the sectioning of the mesocolon, progressing towards the transverse colon, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon sections the greater omentum, opening the lesser sac and releasing the hepatic angle, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon releases the hepatic angle, facilitated by the pneumoperitoneum and previous dissection of the submesocolic space, where a gauze was left as a reference.
The surgeon continues dissection along the right peritoneal gutter, releasing lateral adhesions to the torus fascia, progressing to the fifth step of the procedure.
The surgeon performs ileocecal immobilization, securing the ileocecal region as part of the fifth step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a linear mechanical stapler device to section the mesentery and the terminal ileum, completing the separation of these structures.
The surgeon completes the release of the entire right colon and proceeds to section the transverse colon using a linear mechanical stapler device.
We have opted for intracorporeal anastomosis since we believe it has advantages over extracorporeal.
We don't need to bring the two bowel cut ends out of the cavity, therefore there is less mesotraction and less risk of iatrogenic lesion.
The two cut ends of the bowel are clearly visualized after the section, reducing the likelihood of twisting during the anastomosis.
In the major international series, it's also mentioned faster recovery of bowel movements with consequent shorter length of stay and lower risk of incisional hernia.
The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic mechanical lateral-to-lateral anastomosis using a linear mechanical stapler device with a sixty millimeter vascular load, connecting the cut ends of the bowel.
The surgeon closes the intestinal orifice using a non-resorbable continuous suture, securing the edges of the bowel with precise stitching.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision using an endo bag.
The choice of the extraction site is related to the lower risk of incisional hernia associated with these incisions.
Since we only need to draw one of the cut ends of the specimen instead of the bowel loop as in the exocorporeal anastomosis, we can have smaller incisions.
The surgeon places the greater omentum into the mesenteric defect, visibly positioning it to reduce the risk of internal hernias.
The postoperative period was without intercurrences and the patient was discharged on the fourth day.
Pathology report revealed the PT-IV-A N-II-B with lymphovascular permeation and metastasis in thirteen of thirty-two excised lymph nodes.
The patient underwent adjuvant chemotherapy.

This video proves that, despite a greater technical complexity, it is possible for trainees to perform this procedure safely and with good oncological results if well supervised and with a standardized approach.
